Corrections.PreviousClaim: Buffer Overflow in ExpressionParser::parse — CWE-120 (stack buffer overflow)
Corrections.CorrectedClaim: The ground truth labels this SC-V3-009 as CWE-122 (heap overflow), but the code at lines 366-376 shows expression_buffer_ is stack-allocated via memory::g_memory_pool.allocate(buffer_size_) at line 367, making it a stack buffer overflow (CWE-120). The ground truth's CWE-122 label appears to be incorrect for this code.
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Corrections.CorrectedClaim: The ground truth labels this SC-V3-003 as CWE-416 (Use-After-Free). While the memory leak (CWE-401) is real and is the root cause, the more severe consequence is the Use-After-Free: scratch pointers captured in lambdas become dangling when pool cleanup() frees memory. The previous answer found the leak but not the UAF.
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Corrections.PreviousClaim: TOCTOU Race Condition in validate_config — CWE-367
Corrections.CorrectedClaim: The ground truth SC-V3-019 targets TOCTOU in load_config (lines 543-558 with sleep_for between exists check and file open), not validate_config. The previous answer found a real TOCTOU but in the wrong function.
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Items.Key: remote command injection via admin exec|command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,99|enhanced_calc.cpp|605|608|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|any authenticated admin user can execute arbitrary system commands on the host. this allows full compromise of the system, including data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and persistence.|an authenticated admin user types 'admin exec <arbitrary_command>' in the calculator interface|use a sandboxed command execution library (e.g., execve with explicit argv), validate and whitelist allowed commands, or implement a chroot/jail environment.->remote command injection via admin exec|command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,99|enhanced_calc.cpp|604|606|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|any authenticated admin user can execute arbitrary system commands on the host. this allows full compromise of the system, including data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and persistence.|an authenticated admin user types 'admin exec <arbitrary_command>' in the calculator interface|use a sandboxed command execution library (e.g., execve with explicit argv), validate and whitelist allowed commands, or implement a chroot/jail environment.

buffer overflow in evaluate_expression via strcpy|buffer overflow|cwe-120|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|831|833|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); }|when an expression >= 1024 bytes is entered, strcpy copies it into input_buffer_ (1024 bytes) without bounds checking, causing a stack buffer overflow that can corrupt the stack, enable rop attacks, or crash the process.|user enters an expression longer than 1024 characters (e.g., a long string of numbers and operators)|use strncpy or snprintf with proper bounds checking regardless of input length, or allocate dynamically based on expression size.->buffer overflow in evaluate_expression via strcpy|buffer overflow|cwe-120|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|831|832|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str());|when an expression >= 1024 bytes is entered, strcpy copies it into input_buffer_ (1024 bytes) without bounds checking, causing a stack buffer overflow that can corrupt the stack, enable rop attacks, or crash the process.|user enters an expression longer than 1024 characters (e.g., a long string of numbers and operators)|use strncpy or snprintf with proper bounds checking regardless of input length, or allocate dynamically based on expression size.

toctou race condition in validate_config|time-of-check-time-of-use|cwe-367|medium|0,7|enhanced_calc.cpp|564|568|file_ops::configloader::validate_config|std::string validator_cmd = get_setting("validator_path", "/usr/bin/validator"); std::string config_file = get_setting("config_file", "default.conf"); std::string command = validator_cmd + " " + config_file; int result = system(command.c_str());|between reading the config values and executing system(), an attacker could replace the validator binary at the specified path, causing the program to execute attacker-controlled code. this is especially dangerous since the validator_path comes from user-controlled config.|an attacker replaces the validator binary between config reading and execution|use fork() and execve() with explicit argument vectors to avoid shell interpretation. validate the binary's checksum against a known-good value.->toctou race condition in validate_config|time-of-check-time-of-use|cwe-367|medium|0,7|enhanced_calc.cpp|564|568|file_ops::configloader::validate_config|std::string validator_cmd = get_setting("validator_path", "/usr/bin/validator"); std::string config_file = get_setting("config_file", "default.conf"); std::string command = validator_cmd + " " + config_file; int result = system(command.c_str());|between reading the config values and executing system(), an attacker could replace the validator binary at the specified path, causing the program to execute attacker-controlled code. this is especially dangerous since the validator_path comes from user-controlled config.|an attacker replaces the validator binary between config reading and execution|use fork() and execve() with explicit argument vectors to avoid shell interpretation. validate the binary's checksum against a known-good value.

missing null termination check in safe_string_copy|buffer overflow|cwe-120|high|0,9|enhanced_calc.cpp|200|206|string_utils::safe_string_copy|inline void safe_string_copy(char* dest, const char* src, size_t dest_size) { if (!dest || !src || dest_size == 0) return; size_t src_len = strlen(src); if (src_len > dest_size) src_len = dest_size; memcpy(dest, src, src_len); dest[src_len] = '\0'; }|when src_len >= dest_size, the null terminator is written past the end of the destination buffer. this is a buffer overflow that can corrupt adjacent stack data or enable code execution. the function name suggests safety but it is not safe.|any call where the source string length equals or exceeds the destination buffer size|change line 203 to: 'if (src_len >= dest_size) src_len = dest_size - 1;'

truthAudit.Summary: Previous answer (Run 2) found 8 of 20 true vulnerabilities (found_full), with 2 found_partial on related but differently-characterized issues. 12 true vulnerabilities were completely missed, including integer overflow in factorial, integer underflow in memory pool, unbounded recursion, data race in result cache, and TOCTOU in config loading. Previous answer also made unsupported claims about safe_string_copy buffer overflow and TOCTOU in validate_config (not in ground truth). Key missed areas: arithmetic overflows (SC-V3-002, SC-V3-016), concurrency/data race issues (SC-V3-012, SC-V3-018), and file operation races (SC-V3-019).

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Format String Vulnerability in log_debug_message" ... vulnerability_type: 'Format String Injection' ... cwe: 'CWE-134' ... evidence: 'printf(active_format.c_str(), user_input);'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the format string injection at line 238 where printf uses user-controlled format string active_format with user_input as argument. CWE-134, location, and evidence all match.

Previous answer never mentions factorial, integer overflow, or the fact function (lines 312-320). The fact function uses 'long long result = 1; for (long long i = 2; i <= static_cast<long long>(x); i++) { result *= i; }' which overflows silently for large x values. This was completely absent from the previous answer.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Memory Leak in stage_deferred_history_flush" ... vulnerability_type: 'Memory Leak' ... cwe: 'CWE-401' ... evidence: 'char* scratch = static_cast<char*>(memory::g_memory_pool.allocate(copy_len + 1)); ... thread_pool_->enqueue([scratch, copy_len] { ... })'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the memory leak from stage_deferred_history_flush but characterized it as CWE-401 (memory leak) rather than CWE-416 (use-after-free). The scratch pointer is captured in a lambda, the lambda sleeps 25ms, then the pool's cleanup() at line 884 frees all pool-owned memory — making the scratch pointer dangling when the lambda eventually executes. The UAF is the more severe consequence that the previous answer did not identify.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Command Injection in ConfigLoader::validate_config" ... vulnerability_type: 'Command Injection' ... cwe: 'CWE-78' ... evidence: 'std::string command = validator_cmd + " " + config_file;\n            int result = system(command.c_str());'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the command injection at lines 566-567 where user-controlled config_file and validator_cmd are concatenated and passed to system(). CWE-78, location, and evidence all match.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Buffer Overflow in evaluate_expression via strcpy" ... vulnerability_type: 'Buffer Overflow' ... cwe: 'CWE-120' ... evidence: 'if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) {\n                strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str());'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the buffer overflow at lines 831-832 where strcpy copies input into input_buffer_ (1024 bytes) when expression length >= 1024, bypassing the else branch that uses safe_string_copy. CWE-120, location, and evidence all match.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Use of volatile Instead of std::atomic for ThreadSafeCounter" ... vulnerability_type: 'Race Condition' ... cwe: 'CWE-362' ... evidence: 'volatile long long value_;'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the use of volatile instead of std::atomic for the ThreadSafeCounter value_ at line 478. CWE-362, location, and evidence all match.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Authentication Bypass via EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE" ... vulnerability_type: 'Authentication Bypass' ... cwe: 'CWE-287' ... evidence: 'if (login_attempts_ > 5) {\n                if (input.find("EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE") != std::string::npos) {\n                    authenticated_ = true;'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE bypass at lines 592-598 that grants authenticated_ = true after 5 failed attempts. CWE-287 (Authentication Bypass) is related to CWE-511 (Logic Bomb) but the vulnerability was found in full.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Path Traversal in ConfigLoader" ... vulnerability_type: 'Path Traversal' ... cwe: 'CWE-22' ... evidence: 'std::string full_path = config_dir_ + filename;'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the path traversal at line 543 where filename is concatenated with config_dir_ without validation. CWE-22, location, and evidence all match.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Buffer Overflow in ExpressionParser::parse via strcpy" ... vulnerability_type: 'Buffer Overflow' ... cwe: 'CWE-120' ... evidence: 'if (expr_str.length() > 0) strcpy(expression_buffer_, expr_str.c_str());'
Items.Notes: Previous answer found the vulnerability but classified it as CWE-120 (stack buffer overflow) rather than CWE-122 (heap overflow). The ground truth labels it heap overflow, but the code shows expression_buffer_ is stack-allocated via memory::g_memory_pool.allocate at line 367, making it a stack buffer overflow (CWE-120). The previous answer's CWE-120 classification is arguably more accurate for this code. The vulnerability was found but with a different CWE than the ground truth specifies.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Hardcoded Admin Secret in Source Code" ... vulnerability_type: 'Hardcoded Credential' ... cwe: 'CWE-798' ... evidence: 'constexpr char ADMIN_SECRET[] = "SC_ENT_2025_AUTH";'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the hardcoded admin secret at line 73. CWE-798, location, and evidence all match.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Weak Random Number Generation for Session Tokens" ... vulnerability_type: 'Weak Randomness' ... cwe: 'CWE-338' ... evidence: 'std::mt19937 gen(rd());\n            std::uniform_int_distribution<> dis(1000, 9999);'
Items.Notes: Previous answer correctly identified the weak PRNG at lines 636-639. CWE-338, location, and evidence all match.

Previous answer never mentions static std::tm cached_time, localtime, or thread-unsafe timestamp. The code at lines 230-231 uses a static localtime() result shared across threads, which is a classic data race (CWE-362). This was completely absent from the previous answer.

Previous answer never mentions TEMP, TMPDIR, supercalc_emergency.log, or insecure temporary file handling. The code at lines 624-632 writes to TEMP/TMPDIR via system(command.c_str()) without O_CREAT|O_EXCL flags, making it vulnerable to symlink attacks. This was completely absent.

Previous answer never mentions persistent authentication state, login_attempts_, or session_token_ persistence. The code at lines 579-580, 582 stores authentication state across invocations. This was completely absent.

Previous answer never mentions std::stod(tokens[3]) or unhandled exceptions in variable parsing. The code at line 729 calls std::stod without try-catch, which throws std::invalid_argument or std::out_of_range on malformed input. This was completely absent.

Previous answer never mentions integer underflow, SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES, or remaining calculation. The code at line 133 computes 'size_t remaining = previous_size - size - SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES;' using unsigned size_t arithmetic. If size + SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES > previous_size, remaining wraps to a huge value, causing heap overflow when fragment.data + remaining is computed. This was completely absent.

Previous answer never mentions unbounded recursion, parse_expression, or parse_factor. The parser at lines 381-433 has parse_expression → parse_term → parse_factor → parse_expression with no depth limit, allowing stack exhaustion via deeply nested expressions. This was completely absent.

Previous answer never mentions result_cache_ or data race in the result cache. The code at lines 445-451 reads result_cache_ and writes result_cache_[expression] = value without holding cache_mutex_. The mutex exists (line 442) but is never used to protect cache access. This was completely absent.

Previous answer found a TOCTOU in validate_config (lines 564-568) but not in load_config (lines 543-558). The ground truth SC-V3-019 specifically targets the TOCTOU in load_config where std::filesystem::exists(full_path) at line 544 is followed by sleep_for at line 545, then std::ifstream file(full_path) at line 546. The previous answer's TOCTOU finding was about a different function. This specific vulnerability was missed.

Previous answer never mentions calculate_route_score, integer division by zero, or token_length. The code at line 752 computes 'return 100 / static_cast<int>(token_length);' which divides by zero when token_length is 0 (e.g., for empty input strings). This was completely absent.
